content:
I wonder if we can avoid needing

#![no_std]
#![feature(allocator_api, global_asm)]

on all rust kernel modules, by having a rust_module.rs that have

#![no_std]
#![feature(allocator_api, global_asm)]
include!(env!("RUSTMOD"));

and then compile with RUSTMOD=actual.rs rustc rust_module.rs ... rather than rustc actual.rs ...
Ideally rustc would support a custom prelude -- I would try to reopen the discussion with upstream (e.g. in https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/890).

Rustc has the unstable #[prelude_import] attribute you can apply to a wildcard export. This is how internally the prelude works. It only works for the current crate though. You can't set a global prelude.

Gary Guo said:

Custom prelude wouldn't help features though.

By "custom prelude" I am referring to be able to add anything, similar to the set of things the compiler magically injects today, not just the "actual" std prelude.

bjorn3 said:

Rustc has the unstable #[prelude_import] attribute you can apply to a wildcard export. This is how internally the prelude works. It only works for the current crate though. You can't set a global prelude.

From a quick look, I see prelude_import was added 7 years ago to special case std since shadowing external crates, imports and items became forbidden. Nowadays I can see it allowing a glob import to shadow the marked glob import -- is it used for other things?
Hmm... I am not sure we would like to allow shadowing of the "kernel prelude" via another glob import, in any case.

(There is also the question whether kernel folks would prefer to see something explicit (e.g. an include_raw! that would actually work with inner attributes) vs. something implicit, but given Rust has the concept of the std prelude etc., I think it is reasonable to do it implicitly)
AFAIK #[prelude_import] causes the use to be in scope for every module, not just the one containing the use.

If I macro expand a crate containing only #![no_std] I get:

#![feature(prelude_import)] #![no_std]
#[prelude_import]
use core::prelude::rust_2018::*;
#[macro_use]
extern crate core;
#[macro_use]
extern crate compiler_builtins;

a completely empty crate gives:

#![feature(prelude_import)]
#[prelude_import]
use std::prelude::rust_2018::*;
#[macro_use]
extern crate std;

bjorn3 said:

AFAIK #[prelude_import] causes the use to be in scope for every module, not just the one containing the use.

Thanks! Makes sense. Then it does that + allowing to shadow with a glob import.
Having them in scope for every module sounds useful for us. Perhaps it would be nice to have that without the shadowing, though, but it is a minor bit.
Tested this include! approach and it's sadly not working well because include! is considered an item so the included file cannot have inner attributes or inner doc comments.
